burial.plot_notes: Mount Pleasant Cemetery, Tibbets, Walworth County WI (per Findagrave). Same as parents.

narrative_blocks.header: Second husband made superior-quality sorghum molasses
narrative_blocks.body: After her first husband Levi Hare died, Coris Ann married William Hodges — a Wisconsin manufacturer known locally for his 'superior quality sorghum molasses,' the cane-syrup product that became a mid-nineteenth-century Northern alternative to slavery-produced Southern sugar. The Walworth County Wisconsin sorghum trade reached its peak during the 1860s-1880s as a documented anti-slavery agricultural economy. William Hodges's specific reputation for quality placed him among the regional leaders.

narrative_blocks.header: Two marriages; four children with Levi Hare
narrative_blocks.body: Coris Ann's first marriage to Levi Hare (born May 26, 1837 New York) produced four documented children. After Levi's death she married William Hodges without further documented children. The two-marriage household was typical of nineteenth-century American widows who remarried for economic and social support; her second marriage placed her in the sorghum-molasses trade economy of Walworth County Wisconsin.

Coris Ann Bartlett was born July 21, 1843 in Moriah, Essex County New York — during her father Truman Bartlett's New York years before the family's 1844 migration to Wisconsin. She died April 6, 1927 in Elkhorn, Walworth County Wisconsin at age eighty-three. She is buried at Mount Pleasant Cemetery, Tibbets Wisconsin alongside her parents — a three-generation Wisconsin family cemetery cluster.
